# Enforced provisioners

Enforced provisioners let you define a reusable Packer provisioner definition and apply a specific version of that definition to one or more HCP Packer buckets. Use enforced provisioners when you need selected buckets to consistently run the same provisioning logic, such as security hardening, agent installation, or compliance steps.

<Note>

Cloud UI labels this feature as an enforced provisioner. Some APIs and older internal references use the term enforced block. Both names refer to the same resource.

</Note>

## Before you begin

Make sure your environment meets the following requirements:

- You have access to an HCP Packer registry in the correct organization and project.
- You have permission to create, update, and delete enforced provisioners.
- Your organization and project have the feature enabled.
- You have provisioner content written in the same template format as your Packer configuration, either HCL2 or JSON.

Refer to [Connect to the HCP Packer registry](/packer/docs/hcp) if you need to configure HCP authentication or bucket settings before you start.

## What an enforced provisioner contains

When you create an enforced provisioner, you create the parent resource and its first version at the same time. The following fields are part of that workflow.

| Field | Description |
| ----- | ----------- |
| Name | Human-readable name for the enforced provisioner. |
| Template type | The Packer configuration format. Supported values are `HCL2` and `JSON`. |
| Version | Version label for the first provisioner definition, for example `v1.0.0`. |
| Provisioner content | The provisioner definition that you want to enforce. |
| Additional description | Optional context that explains what the version does and when to use it. |
| Labels | Optional key-value metadata for organizing or filtering provisioners. |

Use version labels that help you distinguish rollout states, such as `v1.0.0`, `v1.1.0`, or `2026-04-hardening`.

## How enforced provisioners work

Enforced provisioners use a version-based lifecycle.

1. Create an enforced provisioner and provide its first version.
2. Add another version when the provisioner content changes.
3. Link a specific version to one or more buckets.
4. Unlink buckets when that version should no longer apply.
5. Delete versions or the full enforced provisioner after you remove all linked buckets.

The following rules affect how version linking behaves across buckets.

- Linking is version-specific. You link a bucket to a specific enforced provisioner version, not only to the parent resource.
- A bucket can have multiple enforced provisioners linked to it.
- A bucket can only link to one version of the same enforced provisioner at a time.
- Updating the linked bucket list for a version replaces the current set of linked buckets for that version.
- Saving an empty linked bucket list unlinks that version from every bucket.
- When the provisioner content changes, create a new version instead of editing the existing version in place.

<Note>

When you move a bucket from one version of an enforced provisioner to another version of the same enforced provisioner, unlink the bucket from the current version first and then link it to the target version.

</Note>

## Create an enforced provisioner in Cloud UI

Use the following workflow when you create an enforced provisioner for the first time.

1. Open HCP Packer in Cloud UI.
2. Select the correct organization and project.
3. Open the target registry.
4. Go to the **Enforced Provisioners** section.
5. Click **Create enforced provisioner**.
6. Enter the provisioner name.
7. Select the template type.
8. Enter the first version label.
9. Paste the provisioner content.
10. Optionally add an additional description and labels.
11. Review the values and create the provisioner.

After the provisioner is created, verify that the new resource appears in the Enforced Provisioners list and that the first version appears in its details view.

_Screenshot placeholder: Enforced Provisioners landing page in Cloud UI._

The following screenshot should show the create form and the field layout.

_Screenshot placeholder: Create Enforced Provisioner form with name, template type, version, content, description, and labels._

## Example provisioner content

The following examples show provisioner content you can paste into Cloud UI when you create a version. If you select `HCL2` in Cloud UI, paste the content from the HCL2 tab. If you select `JSON`, paste the content from the JSON tab.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCL2">

```hcl
provisioner "shell" {
  inline = [
    "apt-get update -y",
    "apt-get install -y curl wget jq"
  ]
}

provisioner "shell" {
  inline = [
    "echo 'Creating app user...'",
    "useradd -m -s /bin/bash appuser",
    "mkdir -p /opt/app",
    "chown appuser:appuser /opt/app"
  ]
}

provisioner "shell" {
  inline = [
    "echo 'Applying security hardening...'",
    "echo 'net.ipv4.ip_forward = 0' >> /etc/sysctl.conf",
    "echo 'Build complete!'"
  ]
}
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="JSON">

```json
{
  "provisioner": [
    {
      "shell": {
        "inline": [
          "apt-get update -y",
          "apt-get install -y curl wget jq"
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "shell": {
        "inline": [
          "echo 'Creating app user...'",
          "useradd -m -s /bin/bash appuser",
          "mkdir -p /opt/app",
          "chown appuser:appuser /opt/app"
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "shell": {
        "inline": [
          "echo 'Applying security hardening...'",
          "echo 'net.ipv4.ip_forward = 0' >> /etc/sysctl.conf",
          "echo 'Build complete!'"
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

Use the template type that matches the builds for the target bucket. HCL2 builds use the HCL2 provisioner blocks. JSON builds use the JSON `provisioner` structure.

## Link an enforced provisioner to one or more buckets

Use the following workflow when you want a specific enforced provisioner version to apply to selected buckets.

1. Open HCP Packer in Cloud UI.
2. Go to the registry that contains the enforced provisioner.
3. Open **Enforced Provisioners**.
4. Select the enforced provisioner you want to use.
5. Open the version you want to link.
6. Open the action to manage linked buckets.
7. Choose one or more bucket names.
8. Save the change.

After you save, verify the bucket list shown for that version. That list is the full source of truth for the version's active links.

_Screenshot placeholder: Enforced Provisioner details page showing available versions._

The following screenshot should show the bucket selection workflow.

_Screenshot placeholder: Manage Linked Buckets dialog with bucket selection._

## Unlink an enforced provisioner from a specific bucket

Use the following workflow when a provisioner version should no longer apply to a single bucket.

1. Open the enforced provisioner in Cloud UI.
2. Open the version currently linked to the bucket.
3. Open the linked bucket management view.
4. Remove the bucket from the selected bucket list.
5. Save the change.
6. Confirm that the bucket no longer appears in the linked bucket list for that version.

If you remove every bucket from the selection and save, that version is unlinked from all buckets.

_Screenshot placeholder: Linked bucket management view showing one bucket being removed._

## Delete an enforced provisioner version

Remove every bucket link from a version before you delete it.

1. Open the enforced provisioner.
2. Open the version you want to delete.
3. Confirm that the version has no linked buckets.
4. Click **Delete version**.
5. Confirm the delete action.

If the version is still linked to one or more buckets, Cloud UI blocks the delete action until you remove those links.

_Screenshot placeholder: Version details page with delete action and no linked buckets._

## Delete an enforced provisioner

Remove every bucket link from every version before you delete the parent provisioner.

1. Open the enforced provisioner.
2. Review all versions and confirm that none of them are linked to any buckets.
3. Click **Delete enforced provisioner**.
4. Confirm the delete action.

If any version is still linked to a bucket, Cloud UI blocks the delete action until you remove all links.

_Screenshot placeholder: Enforced Provisioner summary page with delete confirmation dialog._

## Run builds with enforced provisioners

Packer applies linked enforced provisioners automatically when your build connects to HCP Packer and targets a bucket with active enforced provisioner links.

The following examples show complete build files for HCL2 and JSON templates. The HCL2 example sets the bucket in the template. The JSON example uses environment variables because JSON templates do not support the `hcp_packer_registry` block.
Use `build.pkr.hcl` for the HCL2 example and `build.json` for the JSON example.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCL2">

```hcl
packer {
  required_plugins {
    docker = {
      version = ">= 1.0.0"
      source  = "github.com/hashicorp/docker"
    }
  }
}

source "docker" "ubuntu" {
  image  = "ubuntu:24.04"
  commit = true
}

build {
  name    = "golden-ubuntu"
  sources = ["source.docker.ubuntu"]

  hcp_packer_registry {
    bucket_name = "golden-ubuntu"
  }

  provisioner "shell" {
    inline = [
      "echo 'Install application packages'",
    ]
  }
}
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="JSON">

```json
{
  "builders": [
    {
      "type": "docker",
      "image": "ubuntu:24.04",
      "commit": true
    }
  ],
  "provisioners": [
    {
      "type": "shell",
      "inline": [
        "echo 'Install application packages'"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

Run the build after you configure the required HCP authentication environment variables.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCL2">

```bash
export HCP_CLIENT_ID="example-client-id"
export HCP_CLIENT_SECRET="example-client-secret"

packer build build.pkr.hcl
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="JSON">

```bash
export HCP_CLIENT_ID="example-client-id"
export HCP_CLIENT_SECRET="example-client-secret"
export HCP_PACKER_BUCKET_NAME="golden-ubuntu"

packer build build.json
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

When the referenced bucket has an enforced provisioner version linked to it, Packer injects that provisioner automatically during the build.

## Skip enforced provisioners during a build

Use the `-skip-enforcement` flag when you need to bypass enforced provisioners for a build. This operation requires admin privileges.

The following examples use the same HCL2 and JSON files from the previous section.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="HCL2">

```bash
export HCP_CLIENT_ID="example-client-id"
export HCP_CLIENT_SECRET="example-client-secret"

packer build -skip-enforcement build.pkr.hcl
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="JSON">

```bash
export HCP_CLIENT_ID="example-client-id"
export HCP_CLIENT_SECRET="example-client-secret"
export HCP_PACKER_BUCKET_NAME="golden-ubuntu"

packer build -skip-enforcement build.json
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

## Recommended operating pattern

Use the following pattern to manage enforced provisioners safely.

1. Create a new version when the provisioner content changes.
2. Test the new version with the intended buckets.
3. Unlink buckets from the old version.
4. Link those buckets to the new version.
5. Delete unused versions after they are no longer linked anywhere.

## Troubleshooting

### The Enforced Provisioners section is not visible

Check the following items when Cloud UI does not display the feature.

1. Confirm that you are in the correct organization, project, and registry.
2. Confirm that the feature is enabled for your environment.
3. Confirm that your role includes access to enforced provisioner operations.

### A delete action fails

Delete actions fail when the version or parent resource still has active bucket links.

1. Check whether the provisioner version is still linked to one or more buckets.
2. Remove the linked buckets first.
3. Retry the delete action.

### You cannot link a bucket to a new version of the same enforced provisioner

Each bucket can only link to one version of the same enforced provisioner at a time.

1. Check whether the bucket is still linked to an older version of that enforced provisioner.
2. Unlink the bucket from the older version.
3. Retry the link operation on the target version.
